title: Understanding Secure and Usable Gestures for Realtime Motion based Authentication
venue: IEEE Conference on Computer Communications Workshops (INFOCOM WKSHPS): MobiSec 2018

method.input_representation: 6-dimensional time series (ax, ay, az, gx, gy, gz)
method.training_strategy: Template generation by selecting 5 training samples with the smallest average DTW distances

evaluation.context: Achieved by multiple users (U1, U3, U5, U8, U9) during attack sessions in standing posture
Achieved by user U4 in sitting posture during self-test sessions

evaluation_protocol: Users created personalized gestures; system performed enrollment to create templates; evaluation included self-tests and three attack types (Random, Content-Aware, Mimicry) across sitting and standing postures over four visits.

reported_limitations: Multi-stroke gestures are more difficult to repeat reliably
Simple hand/arm movement gestures are vulnerable to mimicry attacks
Psychological state of the user can cause dramatic drops in authentication success
Posture (sitting vs standing) affects both usability and security

usability.usability_issues: High complexity in drawing characters in the air leads to high false rejection rates

key_contribution: Empirical study identifying the trade-off between gesture complexity (usability) and mimicry resistance (security)
Demonstrated that 3D behavioral biometrics can be highly resistant to targeted mimicry if the gesture has sufficient variation
main_strength: Included real-time authentication feedback to better simulate real-world usage
Tested against a hierarchy of attack sophistication (Random -> Content-Aware -> Mimicry)
main_weakness: Data collected in a controlled experimental setting
insights.notes_for_review: The paper suggests that the best gestures for authentication are single-stroke with sufficient movement variation and smooth direction/speed changes.
